notes.en: Warm, dry weather favours rapid asexual reproduction. Tender new growth and N-lush plants attract colonies. Indiscriminate broad-spectrum insecticides suppress natural enemies, triggering aphid resurgence.

economic_threshold.en: Visual monitoring or yellow water/sticky traps. Spot-apply at initial infestation; direct spray to shoots and leaf undersides.

description.en: Soft-bodied sap-sucking insects found in dense colonies on shoots, leaf undersides, and flower buds. Feeding causes leaf curling, yellowing, stunting and honeydew with sooty mould. More importantly, aphids transmit key viruses — bean common mosaic virus (BCMV), bean yellow mosaic virus (BYMV), groundnut rosette, potato virus Y — making vector management critical. **DOA 2019 pesticide recommendations**: seed treatments with Imidacloprid 70% WS or Thiamethoxam 70% WS; foliar options include Chlorantraniliprole 20% + Thiamethoxam 20% WG, Thiocyclam, and Buprofezin 10% WP. Biological control via syrphid flies, ladybird beetles, lacewings, and braconid wasps — encourage with flowering border crops and avoid broad-spectrum pesticides.

description.quote: Sri Lanka Department of Agriculture's pesticide recommendations include several options for managing aphids (Aphis craccivora and Myzus persicae), such as seed treatments with Imidacloprid 70% WS or Thiamethoxam 70% WS, and foliar sprays with Chlorantraniliprole 20% + Thiamethoxam 20% WG, Thiocyclam, and Buprofezin 10% WP

economic_threshold.quote: Aphid populations on plants should be monitored visually or by placing yellow water traps in the field, with spot application being desirable at the initial stage of infestation

favored_conditions.quote: Aphids on groundnut-Sri Lanka: Aphis craccivora

notes.en: Nocturnal adults; larvae tunnel in corms and pseudo-stems. Accumulated crop residue and dense ratoon mats favour outbreaks.

description.en: Destructive weevil of banana and ash plantain worldwide; larvae tunnel galleries through the rhizome/corm, weakening the mat, reducing bunch weight, and predisposing plants to wind toppling. **Management** centres on aggregation-pheromone mass trapping: **(1S,3R,5R,7S)-sordidin** (commercial: Cosmolure+, Sordilure) combined with banana-tissue kairomones gives synergistic attraction — pseudostem-only traps catch **18× fewer weevils** than pheromone-kairomone combinations. Deploy 4–8 pheromone traps per ha. Complement with: (a) clean planting material (corm-paring to remove grubs); (b) sanitation — chop and dry harvested pseudo-stems; (c) resistant/tolerant cultivars; (d) entomopathogens *Beauveria bassiana* and *Metarhizium* soil drench.

description.quote: Cosmopolites sordidus produces an aggregation pheromone that attracts both males and females, which has been identified and synthesized and is being recommended as an effective agent in the trapping and control of the weevil. The male-produced aggregation pheromone of C. sordidus has been identified as (1S,3R,5R,7S)-sordidin. The synthetic pheromone, named sordidin or Cosmolure+ (depending on manufacturers), has been found to be successful in mass trappings; capturing 18 times more weevils than conventional pseudostem traps in Uganda

notes.en: Persists year-round in Sri Lanka's brinjal-growing areas since brinjal is often cultivated continuously. Overlapping generations and short life-cycle drive rapid build-up.

description.en: **The major pest of eggplant in South Asia** including Sri Lanka, where brinjal is the country's second most extensively-grown vegetable after ash plantain. Larvae bore into young shoots (causing dead-heart wilting) and into developing fruits (rendering fruit unmarketable). **Pheromone-based IPM is the DOA-recommended primary strategy**: Sri Lankan research confirmed (E)-11-hexadecenyl acetate (E11-16:Ac) as the major female sex pheromone component, best deployed with 1–10% of (E)-11-hexadecen-1-ol (E11-16:OH) — blends in this ratio catch more males than E11-16:Ac alone. Additional IPM tools: (a) remove and destroy wilted shoots and bored fruits weekly; (b) mass-trapping with pheromone lures; (c) sex-pheromone mating disruption; (d) Bt-based biopesticides; (e) selective insecticides only at damage threshold; (f) crop rotation out of Solanaceae.

description.quote: The brinjal fruit and shoot borer, Leucinodes orbonalis is the major pest of eggplant in South Asia. Brinjal plant is an economically important plant grown in Sri Lanka and other Asian countries, and in Sri Lanka, it covers the second large extent after ash plantain.

favored_conditions.quote: Analysis of female pheromone gland extracts confirmed (E)-11-hexadecenyl acetate (E11-16:Ac) as the major pheromone component with 0.8 to 2.8% of the related (E)-11-hexadecen-1-ol (E11-16:OH), as previously reported from Sri Lanka. In field trials conducted in India, blends containing between 1 and 10% E11-16:OH caught more male L. orbonalis than E11-16:Ac alone.

economic_threshold.en: DOA guideline: 2 BPH per hill at booting stage or 5 per hill at heading stage, adjusted for natural enemy (spider) presence.

notes.en: Higher moisture levels and indiscriminate insecticide use during the vegetative stage known to trigger BPH outbreaks; most damaging during late vegetative and reproductive stages.

description.en: Heavy infestations produce hopper burn — leaves dry and turn brown after feeding, with patches of burned plants often lodged. Also transmits grassy stunt and ragged stunt viruses, compounding losses.

description.quote: Heavy infestations produce symptoms of hopper burn. Leaves dry and turn brown after insect feeding, and patches of burned plants are often lodged. Acts as a disease vector for grassy stunt and ragged stunt virus diseases.

economic_threshold.quote: booting stage: 2 per hill; heading stage: 5 per hill threshold

favored_conditions.quote: Indiscriminate use of insecticides during vegetative stage known to cause BPH outbreaks. Higher moisture levels support multiplication

notes.en: Hot, dry conditions favour rapid population build-up. Short generation time (14–20 days) allows multiple overlapping generations per season.

economic_threshold.en: ETL set at 2 thrips per leaf in Sri Lanka. Sticky traps help detect thrips up to a month before visible on plants — hang yellow sticky cards at canopy top; inspect weekly.

description.en: Presently one of the most important causal agents of the chilli leaf curl complex in Sri Lanka. Nymphs and adults rasp leaf, flower, and young-fruit surfaces, causing silvery scarring, leaf curling upward, bud abortion, and in severe infestations complete defoliation and total crop loss. Confirmed vector of tospoviruses. Management: (a) monitoring with yellow sticky traps; (b) organic control with NSKE 5%, neem cake (0.5 t/ha), vermicompost (2.5 t/ha) and neemazal 10,000 ppm applied 2/5/7/11 weeks after transplanting; (c) biological control — *Orius laevigatus* and *Amblyseius swirskii* (predatory mite) are effective on chilli in Sri Lanka; (d) **do NOT use pyrethroids** — they are toxic to natural enemies and not effective against chilli thrips.

description.quote: Chilli thrips, Scirtothrips dorsalis, are presently considered as one of the most important causal agents of the chilli leaf curl complex in Sri Lanka. Severe infestation creates the tender leaves and buds of chilli plants brittle and complete defoliation and total crop loss is expected. Economic Threshold Level (ETL) set at two thrips leaf-1.

economic_threshold.quote: Economic Threshold Level (ETL) set at two thrips leaf-1. Sticky traps may help to detect thrips up to a month before they are seen on plants

favored_conditions.quote: Orius laevigatus and Amblyseius swirskii effectively controlled S. dorsalis on chilli pepper in Sri Lanka

notes.en: Drought stress and continuous coconut monocrop with uncleaned fallen debris amplify outbreaks. Cyclical population dynamics with natural-enemy crash phases.

description.en: **Officially declared a pest of coconut in Sri Lanka in 1924**, making it among the longest-regulated insect pests on record — outbreaks were legally reportable to the agricultural authority from that date. The Coconut Research Institute (CRI) became the leading authority in 1956 and documented outbreaks 1965–1985. First described in India and Sri Lanka in the mid-19th century. Larvae feed gregariously on the underside of coconut fronds, spinning silken galleries and consuming parenchyma between veins; severe infestation causes scorched brown appearance to the canopy, reduced photosynthesis, delayed flowering and up to 45% nut-yield loss in outbreak years. **CRI management emphasises non-chemical IPM** — crop diversification under coconut, pheromone-based monitoring, and native/introduced natural enemies (parasitoid wasps *Goniozus nephantidis* and *Bracon brevicornis*, predators, entomopathogenic fungi). Chemical control is a last resort due to pollinator and natural-enemy risk.

description.quote: Opisina arenosella was officially declared a pest in Sri Lanka in 1924, when it became a legal requirement to report any outbreaks to the agricultural authority. The Coconut Research Institute (CRI) became the leading agricultural authority in 1956, and have documents of all reported outbreaks between 1965 & 1985. Opisina arenosella was first described in India and Sri Lanka during the mid-19th century. For Opisina arenosella, the work primarily focuses on non-chemical integrated pest management (IPM) methods and prevention strategies. These include crop diversification, pheromone-based monitoring and the use of (native, exotic) natural enemies for BHC biological control.

notes.en: Invaded Sri Lanka in the late 1990s and now occurs in all main coconut-growing areas. Hot, relatively dry conditions favour population build-up.

description.en: Microscopic mites colonize the perianth under the bracts of young developing coconuts, scarring the epidermis. Affected nuts show triangular whitish patches progressing to brown corky lesions, nut stunting, and premature nut drop. Sri Lankan management centres on: (a) low-toxicity chemical sprays — 20% palm oil + 0.5% sulphur mixture has proven successful and cost-effective; (b) biological control with the predacious mite *Neoseiulus baraki* (indigenous natural enemy); (c) field sanitation — collect and destroy fallen infested nuts.

description.quote: Coconut mite (Aceria guerreronis) is a major pest of coconut in Sri Lanka, and after its invasion in late 1990s, has widely spread to all main coconut growing areas in the country. The most effective control methods are chemical control using low toxic chemicals (20% palm oil and 0.5% sulphur mixture) and biological control using the predaceous mite Neoseiulus baraki, which were proven to be successful and cost effective methods.

notes.en: Warm, dry weather — classic up-country wet/intermediate zone cabbage-growing season conditions (Nuwara Eliya, Badulla). Short generation time (14–21 days) and high insecticide-resistance potential.

description.en: The key global pest of cabbage and other brassicas; one of the most significant cabbage pests in Sri Lanka's up-country cabbage-growing areas. Larvae window-feed on young leaves, producing irregular holes and a skeletonized appearance; heavy infestation reduces head quality and marketability. **High insecticide-resistance risk** — farmers over-relying on chemicals has driven resistance. **Sri Lanka IPM research** (Tropical Agricultural Research & Extension, 2022) evaluates physical barriers (nets, covers) as eco-friendly alternatives. Key management: (a) crop rotation away from brassicas; (b) trap crops (mustard attractants); (c) biocontrol — *Trichogramma chilonis* (egg parasitoid), *Cotesia plutellae* (larval parasitoid), *Chrysoperla carnea* (lacewing predator); (d) selective insecticides — chlorantraniliprole, spinosad, B. thuringiensis (Bt); avoid chlorpyrifos + pyrethroids in rotation to delay resistance; (e) physical exclusion nets on nursery beds and early-growth crops.

description.quote: in Sri Lanka, diamondback moth is one of the key pests of cabbage that causes significant yield loss, with farmers heavily using agro-chemicals to control it while ignoring alternative non-chemical eco-friendly pest management strategies. Research has evaluated the combined effectiveness of physical pest management strategies to suppress the diamondback moth population in cabbage as eco-friendly alternatives.

favored_conditions.quote: Common natural enemies of the moth include the parasitoids Trichogramma chilonis and Cotesia plutella and the predator Chrysoperla carnea, a lacewing

notes.en: First detected in Sri Lanka in all major maize-growing areas in October 2018. Highly mobile — adults fly long distances and colonise new regions rapidly.

economic_threshold.en: Established monitoring via pheromone traps; action threshold typically 5% whorl damage or 1 larva per plant in early-whorl maize.

description.en: Larvae feed on whorls of maize, cutting windowpane holes in leaves and producing sawdust-like frass; severe infestations kill the growing point. DOA + FAO response in Sri Lanka uses integrated pest management: pheromone-lure traps for monitoring and mass trapping (FAO distributed), Farmer Field School extension, natural-enemy conservation (predators, parasitoids, entomopathogens), and biological control over pesticide-first responses. The program reduced FAW-affected area to 144 ha out of 104,000 ha planted by the season ending April 2019 — <0.2% of area with <2% yield loss.

description.quote: The Fall Armyworm (FAW) or Spodoptera frugiperda was first detected in all major maize growing areas in Sri Lanka in October 2018. FAO distributed pheromone traps which are used to detect the presence and build-up of FAW. During the last season ending in April, out of the total 104,000 hectares of maize cultivated, only 144 hectares were affected by FAW with the yield loss recorded at less than 2 percent.

notes.en: Become especially serious during dry periods and under protected cultivation. Overuse of broad-spectrum insecticides eliminates parasitoid wasps and triggers leaf-miner resurgence.

description.en: *Liriomyza sativae* is recorded in Sri Lanka with very high field infestation rates — 89% on tomato and 98% on mung bean in surveys. Adults are small black-and-yellow flies; females puncture leaves to feed and lay eggs. Larvae tunnel between the upper and lower leaf epidermis, creating the characteristic serpentine whitish mines; heavy mining reduces photosynthesis, causes leaf drop, and stunts plants. **DOA-recommended management**: yellow sticky traps, row covers to exclude adult flies, removal of alternate weed hosts, prompt destruction of infested leaves. Chemical options cited by DOA: Azadirachtin 1% EC at 16 ml per 16 L water, Abamectin 18 g/L EC at 9.6 ml per 16 L, neem seed water extract at 640 g per 16 L; Spinetoram has shown the highest reduction 7 days after application.

description.quote: Liriomyza sativae prefers hosts in the Cucurbitaceae, Fabaceae and Solanaceae families. Tomato showed 89% infestation and mung bean 98% infestation in field studies. DOA recommends Azadiractin 1% EC at 16ml/16 liters of water, Abamectin 18g/l EC at 9.6 ml/16 liters of water, and neem seed water extract at 640g/16 liters of water. Insecticidal effects showed the highest reduction values were observed after 7 DOA for Spinetoram

notes.en: Populations peak during flowering and early fruit-setting season. Dense canopies and high humidity accelerate outbreaks.

description.en: Three globally economically-important species — *Amritodus atkinsoni*, *Idioscopus clypealis*, *I. niveosparsus* — attack mango mainly at flowering and early fruiting. Nymphs and adults suck phloem sap from twigs, inflorescences, young leaves and fruitlets, causing flower shrivelling, bud abortion and **50%+ yield loss** under heavy pressure. Honeydew secretion promotes sooty mould that further reduces photosynthesis. Management: (a) **Imidacloprid** highest efficacy — 92.5% reduction at 72 h after treatment, 88.6% overall reduction, relatively low toxicity to natural enemies; (b) neem-oil (azadirachtin) reduces 56–60% while preserving beneficials — suitable for organic/eco-friendly programmes; (c) biocontrol predators — *Mallada boninensis*, *Chrysopa lacciperda* (lacewings); egg-parasitoid *Polynema* sp.; (d) canopy pruning for airflow; (e) sanitation — remove fallen inflorescences.

economic_threshold.en: Monitor flower panicles weekly during flowering; treatment warranted at >5 hoppers per panicle.

description.quote: Three species – Amritodus atkinsoni, Idioscopus clypealis, and Idioscopus niveosparsus – are of global economic importance, attacking trees mainly during flowering and early fruiting. Both nymphs and adults suck phloem sap from twigs, inflorescences, tender leaves, and young fruits. On heavily infested trees, crop losses of 50% or more have been recorded. Imidacloprid showed the highest efficacy in percentage of reduction of hopper population (92.50 ± 9.02) at 72 hours after treatment. Neem oil (azadirachtin-based) achieves 56-60% reduction while preserving natural enemy populations

notes.en: Dry, warm weather during onion/leek bulbing phase amplifies populations. Weed grasses and cereal volunteers are key alternate hosts.

description.en: The primary thrips pest of Sri Lanka's onion (big onion) and leek up-country crops. Adults and nymphs rasp leaf surfaces producing silvery scarring that reduces photosynthesis; also transmits iris yellow spot virus (IYSV). Damage aligns with bulb initiation — significant yield impact. **Management**: (a) **spinosad + neem oil combination spray** — DOA-cited IPM protocol; reduces populations and increases yields; (b) **flower-strip habitat manipulation** — reduced thrips 50% with 25% yield uplift in research trials; (c) yellow/blue sticky traps at canopy height; (d) avoid over-N fertilisation; (e) in polytunnels, Amblyseius predatory mites; (f) resistant cultivars where trialed ('Rossa di Milano' outperforms 'Bradley').

description.quote: Onion thrips (Thrips tabaci) is a well-known onion pest worldwide that causes both direct and indirect damage to onion by feeding and ovipositing on leaves. Onions sprayed with spinosad + neem oil had fewer T. tabaci and produced higher yields than onions that were unsprayed. Habitat manipulation techniques, particularly the use of flower strips, reduced thrips populations by up to 50% and increased onion yields by 25%. The cultivar Rossa di Milano had relatively fewer adult and larval thrips populations compared with Bradley.

description.en: The most virulent insect pest in Sri Lanka's commercial fruit industry. Females lay eggs beneath fruit skin; larvae tunnel through flesh causing decay and premature fruit drop. Attacks 300+ fruit species worldwide. Management in Sri Lanka centres on methyl eugenol (ME) male-lure traps for monitoring and mass trapping; ME-baited field traps are the standard DOA-recommended detection tool. Continuous ME application significantly reduces fruit infestation versus untreated sites.

notes.en: Populations peak during fruiting seasons of host crops. Warm, humid conditions and fallen/overripe fruit accelerate breeding.

description.quote: Fruit flies (Diptera: Tephritidae) are considered as the most virulent insect pest group in the fruit industry worldwide, and methyl-eugenol field traps have been used to collect adult fruit flies in Sri Lanka.

notes.en: Warm, dry conditions favour rapid population build-up. Spreads on infested planting material, farm equipment, and wind.

description.en: An invasive alien pest first recorded in Sri Lanka in early 2008 in Colombo and Gampaha districts; originates from Mexico / Central America. Polyphagous across 40+ plant species with papaya as primary host and mango, rambutan, guava, annona as secondary hosts. Colonies of tiny white-waxy mealybugs encrust stems, leaves and fruits, secreting honeydew that supports sooty mould. Severe infestation stunts papaya trees and renders fruit unmarketable. **Landmark Sri Lankan biocontrol success**: DOA imported three parasitoid wasps from Puerto Rico in 2008–2009 — *Acerophagus papayae* (the dominant effective agent), *Anagyrus loecki*, and *Pseudleptomastix mexicana*. *Acerophagus papayae* now plays the major role in sustained suppression of *P. marginatus* populations across the dry zone.

description.quote: A previously unrecorded papaya mealybug species, Paracoccus marginatus, was discovered in early 2008 in the western provincial districts Colombo and Gampaha in Sri Lanka. This invasive alien species originated from Mexico and/or Central America. A biocontrol programme was implemented with the introduction of three natural enemies—Acerophagus papayae, Anagyrus loeki, and Pseudleptomastix mexicana—imported from Puerto Rico in selected regions in 2008 and 2009. Acerophagus papayae was found playing a major role in managing populations of P. marginatus.

notes.en: Highly polyphagous invasive across Sri Lanka fruit crops. Currently at reduced population density thanks to introduced hymenopteran parasitoids.

description.en: Highly polyphagous invasive mealybug recorded in Sri Lankan fruit crop surveys as **medium-importance** on papaya (primary host), mango, rambutan, anona, and guava (secondary hosts). Injects toxic saliva causing distorted bunchy-top-like symptoms on shoots. **Management**: (a) **biological control** with introduced hymenopteran parasitoids (similar approach to papaya mealybug with *Anagyrus kamali* and *Cryptolaemus montrouzieri*) — currently keeping populations low in Sri Lanka; (b) **chemical** — acetamiprid, flupyradifurone, fenpropathrin and bifenthrin most toxic to nymphs; (c) **postharvest** hot-water dips as quarantine treatment on exported fruit; (d) prune infested shoots and destroy; (e) ant control (ants tend mealybugs).

description.quote: Maconellicoccus hirsutus, commonly known as pink hibiscus mealybug, is a highly polyphagous, invasive pest in many parts of the world. This species was identified among mealybug species collected in Sri Lanka, and M. hirsutus was of medium importance on three fruit crops. Papaya is its primary host but mango, rambutan, anona and guava serve as secondary hosts. The pest is present at lower population levels than in previous years, probably due to the action of introduced hymenopteran parasitoids.

favored_conditions.quote: Acetamiprid, flupyradifurone, fenpropathrin, and bifenthrin were the most toxic insecticides to mealybug nymphs

notes.en: Adults attack wounded or young palms, laying eggs in leaf axils or wound sites. Larvae bore into the crown, causing internal damage that is often undetectable externally until advanced.

description.en: A major pest of coconut throughout Sri Lanka. Managed via integrated pest management: (a) mass trapping with ferrugineol (aggregation pheromone) + food bait traps — CRI research shows **5 L open plastic buckets baited with ferrugineol-pentanol hung on palm stems at 1.5 m catch significantly more adult weevils than other trap designs, with lure effectiveness up to 12 weeks**; (b) farm sanitation (remove dead palms and decaying organic matter); (c) preventive chemical treatment of crown wounds; (d) filling frond axils of young palms with an insecticide-sand mixture; (e) curative treatment in early-stage infestations. The CRI has also developed a 'Red Weevil Detector' — a low-frequency acoustic amplifier that detects larval feeding sounds inside the palm.

description.quote: mass trapping adult weevils using ferrugineol-based food baited traps, crop and field sanitation, preventive chemical treatments of wounds, filling frond axils of young palms with mixture of insecticide and sand, and curative treatment of infested palms. 5L open plastic buckets baited with ferrugineol-pentanol and hung on coconut palm stems at a height of 1.5 meters catch significantly more adult weevils than other trap designs, with the effectiveness of these lures persisting for up to 12 weeks under field conditions. The Red Weevil Detector, developed by the Coconut Research Institute, functions as a low-frequency amplifier that can detect the sounds produced by feeding larvae within the palm.

notes.en: Warm dry weather at germination to early-vegetative stage; damage heaviest on cotyledons and young true leaves.

description.en: Introduced invasive pest in Sri Lanka. Adults are reddish-orange beetles 5–8 mm long; strongly prefers pumpkin (*Cucurbita maxima*) but also feeds on squash, melon, cucumber, watermelon, and sponge gourd. **Adults chew leaves, buds and flowers (defoliating young plants); grubs bore into roots and damage stems and fruits lying on the soil.** Integrated management: (a) **botanical** — neem (NSKE 5%) sprays give significant population reduction while preserving natural enemies; (b) **entomopathogens** — *Beauveria bassiana* and *Metarhizium anisopliae* applied as biopesticides; (c) **mechanical** — yellow sticky traps; (d) **cultural** — avoid continuous cucurbit cropping; (e) chemical (carbaryl dust) only as last resort. A combination of carbaryl dust + NSKE + yellow sticky traps is most effective.

description.quote: The red pumpkin beetle (Aulacophora foveicollis Lucas) is a leaf beetle of the family Chrysomelidae characterized by an elongate body measuring 5-8 mm in length with a reddish-orange head and thorax. The beetle has been introduced to Sri Lanka and Southeast Asia. A. foveicollis favours pumpkin (Cucurbita maxima) over other cucurbit crops, but will also feed on squash, melon, cucumber, watermelon and sponge gourd. They feed on leaves, buds and flowers and can defoliate plants; the grubs bore into roots and damage stems and fruits that lie on the soil.

notes.en: Damage is high in wet, humid weather. High infestation in Dry and Intermediate zones during Maha; in Wet zone during Yala. Severity depends on crop growth stage at attack.

description.en: Larvae feed on rice buds and induce 'silver' or 'onion' shoot galls that terminate tiller development and reduce yield. Cultivation of resistant varieties in endemic areas is the most economical management. Biotype I resistance: Bg 276-5, Bg 400-1, Bg 380, Bg 450, Bg 300. Biotype I+II resistance: Bg 304, Bg 305, Bg 357, Bg 359, Bg 360.

description.quote: larvae feed on rice buds, causing gall formation called a 'Silver' or 'Onion' shoot. These galls terminate the tiller development and hence affect rice yield. Biotype I resistant: Bg 276-5, Bg 400-1, Bg 380, Bg 450, Bg 300. Biotype I & II resistant: Bg 304, Bg 305, Bg 357, Bg 359, Bg 360

favored_conditions.quote: Damage is high in wet humid weather. High infestation occurs in dry and intermediate zones during the maha season, and in wet zones during yala

notes.en: Heavy pre-monsoon / early-monsoon rain followed by abnormally low precipitation + minimum day-night temperature differential + high humidity favour outbreaks. Grassy weeds around fields are critical alternate hosts.

description.en: Recorded in Sri Lanka's rice-growing regions along with Myanmar, India, Thailand, Cambodia, Nepal, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, China, and Pakistan. Adults scrape the upper leaf epidermis leaving only the lower layer ('window-paning'); grubs mine between leaf layers producing translucent whitish tunnels. Under heavy pressure plants lose vigour and tiller fewer panicles. Management: (a) **avoid over-fertilisation** (N-lush plants are more susceptible); (b) close plant spacing gives high enough leaf density to tolerate hispa; (c) **cut and destroy shoot tips** to remove eggs and clip infested shoots 'and bury in mud' — **reduces grub populations 75–92%**; (d) biological control — parasitoid wasps on eggs and larvae, a reduviid predator on adults, three fungal entomopathogens on adults; (e) weed control to remove alternate hosts; (f) mechanical adult collection with sweep nets; (g) selective insecticides only at threshold.

description.quote: Rice hispa is known in Myanmar, India, Thailand, Cambodia, Nepal, Indonesia, Laos, Malaysia, China, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka. Rice hispa scrapes the upper surface of leaf blades leaving only the lower epidermis and tunnels through the leaf tissues.

favored_conditions.quote: The presence of grassy weeds in and near rice fields as alternate hosts harbor and encourage the pest to develop, and heavily fertilized fields also encourage the damage. Heavy rains, especially in premonsoon or earliest monsoon periods, followed by abnormally low precipitation, minimum day-night temperature differential for a number of days, and high RH are favorable. Cutting shoot tips to prevent egg laying, and clipping and burying shoots in the mud can reduce grub populations by 75−92%

notes.en: Warm, wet, shaded canopies (high N, dense stands) favour outbreaks. Broad-spectrum insecticide overuse eliminates *Trichogrammatoidea* parasitoids and triggers resurgence.

description.en: **A major rice pest in Sri Lanka, affecting ~10% of the rice crop annually.** Larvae fold a rice leaf lengthwise around themselves with silk threads and scrape chlorophyll from inside, creating long whitish-translucent streaks. Severe infestation in booting to flowering stage reduces yield significantly. **DOA (2010) recommends Methoxyfenozide 240 g/L SC**; biological control is preferred — the egg parasitoid *Trichogrammatoidea bactrae* is present in Sri Lankan rice fields and destroys the pest at the egg stage. Integrated management: healthy cultivation (avoid excess N), ecological regulation (preserve natural enemies), behavioural regulation (pheromone monitoring), selective insecticide application only at damage threshold.

description.quote: Rice leaf folder, Cnaphalocrocis medinalis is a major pest in many rice growing areas of Sri Lanka. In Sri Lanka, 10% of rice crop is affected annually by the RLF. Trichogrammatoidea bactrae present in rice fields in Sri Lanka is an egg parasitoid of rice leaf folder.

description.en: Nymphs and adults suck developing grains, causing empty or partially filled grains. DOA estimates a 3–5% yield reduction in Sri Lanka. Natural enemies — especially the egg parasitoid *Gryon nixoni* — help suppress populations.

description.quote: Sucks the developing grains causing empty or partially filled grains. Both nymphs and adults damage the grains. The pest reportedly reduces rice yield by an estimated 3-5% in Sri Lanka. Gryon nixoni being noted as the most prevalent egg parasitoid in Sri Lanka.

notes.en: Severe in Sri Lanka's Eastern Province (Ampara) sugarcane plantations since 2016 with increasing infestation trend.

description.en: A major sugarcane borer in Sri Lanka — attacks both plant cane and ratoon crops. Larvae bore into internodes, hollowing stems and reducing cane quality and sugar recovery. Alongside *Sesamia inferans* (shoot borer) it is a principal borer pest of SRI-registered sugarcane. **Management**: (a) **Fipronil 0.3% granular (Prince) at 18 kg/ha** — Sri Lankan field trials showed significant effectiveness against internode borer; (b) **Chlorantraniliprole 0.4% w/w G** effective on shoot and stalk damage in both plant and ratoon crops; (c) sex-pheromone trapping — SRI + international collaboration 2019–20 optimised Sri Lankan trap design; (d) *Trichogramma chilonis* egg parasitoid releases; (e) sanitation — destroy infested stubble after harvest; (f) resistant-variety selection.

description.quote: Sugarcane borers (Chilo sacchariphagus indicus and Sesamia inferans) are a major pest group in sugarcane in Sri Lanka. Chilo sacchariphagus (Internode Borer) causes severe damage to the sugarcane crop and an increasing trend of infestation has been recorded in sugarcane plantations in Ampara district in Eastern province of Sri Lanka since 2016. Chlorantraniliprole 0.4% (w/w) G showed a significant effect on shoot and stalk damages due to borers during both plant and ratoon crops compared to the control.

favored_conditions.quote: Fipronil in its granular form of Prince at the recommended dosage of 18kg/ha is most suitable for controlling the internode borer Chilo sacchariphagus

notes.en: Populations rise sharply in warm weather and decline markedly once monsoon rains arrive. Under optimal conditions up to 22 overlapping generations per year.

economic_threshold.en: In Sri Lanka Oligonychus coffeae and Brevipalpus spp. can cause 0.6–5 % yield reduction; some TRI-released clones have inherent resistance.

description.en: Major mite pest of tea (*Camellia sinensis*). Nymphs and adults lacerate leaf cells on the upper surface, producing characteristic minute reddish-brown scars; under severe infestation leaves turn red, photosynthesis and quality drop, and yield falls. Integrated management: (a) **host-plant resistance** — TRI-resistant clones reduce populations at source; (b) botanical extracts (neem and Trichilia derivatives) match or exceed propargite efficacy; (c) microbial biocontrol — *Purpureocillium lilacinum*, *Metarhizium robertsii*, *Aspergillus niger*, *Pseudomonas fluorescens*, *P. putida*; (d) natural predators — green lacewings, ladybirds, phytoseiid mites; (e) chemical acaricides as last resort with strict resistance management.

economic_threshold.quote: In Sri Lanka O. coffeae and Brevipalpus spp. could cause 0.6–5 % reduction of yield. Additionally, some tea clones in Sri Lanka are also resistant to O. coffeae.

description.quote: Nymphs and adults of RSM lacerate cells, producing minute characteristic reddish brown marks on the upper surface of mature leaves, which turn red in severe cases of infestation. Their number increases as the weather warms up and decreases markedly once rains set in. Under optimal conditions there may be 22 overlapping generations in a year.

favored_conditions.quote: Purpureocillium lilacinum, Metarhizium robertsii, Aspergillus niger, Pseudomonas fluorescens, and Pseudomonas putida are highly effective against TRSM without causing any harm to the nontarget beneficial insects

notes.en: Attacks tea across 150–1400 m elevation, though also found outside this band. Stressed or poorly pruned bushes are more vulnerable.

description.en: **The most serious pest of tea in Sri Lanka** per TRI (recognised as part of the *Euwallacea fornicatus* species complex). Female beetles bore into stems and create galleries where they farm symbiotic *Fusarium* ambrosia fungus as larval food. Two injury types: (1) primary — gallery construction weakens stems and causes **branch breakage**; (2) secondary — associated wood rot debilitates the bush, reducing yield and shortening productive life. TRI Advisory Circular I-4 is the national reference. **Management**: (a) **sanitation of infected hosts** is the most recommended step — prune out infested branches and burn; (b) avoid spread via infested planting material; (c) chemical control must be truly prophylactic — historical use of persistent organochlorines (DDT, dieldrin, aldrin, telodrin) caused severe secondary-pest outbreaks and is now prohibited; (d) plant-semiochemical research on attracting adults for mass trapping is ongoing; (e) field hygiene — remove shade-tree prunings that may host alternate beetles.

description.quote: Shot-hole borer (SHB), Xyleborus fornicatus Eichh. (Coleoptera: Scolytidae), is the most serious pest of tea in Sri Lanka. This insect causes two types of injury. The primary injury is caused during the constructions of galleries in the stems, which results in branch breakages, whereas the secondary injury occurs from wood rot, leading to debilitation of the tea plant. It has become a problem in tea grown in the range 150 -1400 m above sea level

favored_conditions.quote: The use of utilization of persistent insecticides such as DDT, dieldrin, aldrin and telodrin to control SHB has caused serious secondary pest outbreaks

notes.en: Seasonal pest — larvae damage accelerates during dry weather when natural-enemy population crashes. Worst in up-country and Uva regions.

description.en: **Economically important pest of tea in Sri Lanka's up-country and Uva regions** (TRI Talawakelle advisory). Larvae fold young tea leaves with silk and feed inside, causing plucking-yield loss during dry seasons. The parasitoid **Macrocentrus homonae** (Braconidae) has historically provided effective biological control, but **hyperparasitism rates of 35–52%** on *Macrocentrus* in recent Sri Lankan plantation surveys have eroded its impact. Current IPM per TRI: (a) regular and hard plucking to physically remove larval generations; (b) selective insecticides in hot-spot plantations; (c) monitoring for hyperparasite pressure on *Macrocentrus*; (d) emerging alternative insecticides under evaluation.

description.quote: Tea tortrix, Homona coffearia Nietner (Lepidoptera: Tortricidae), is a seasonal pest of tea in Sri Lanka. It is an economically important insect pest of tea in the regions of up country and Uva regions in Sri Lanka, with larvae feeding on young tea leaves of pluckable tea shoots and causing considerable yield loss during dry weather. The parasite, Macrocentrus homonae Nixon (Hymenoptera: Braconidae), controls the tortrix populations, but the efficiency of Macrocentrus has been reduced on account of hyperparasites in recent times. Hyperparasitism levels varied during study periods, with average values observed ranging from 35% to 52%

notes.en: Adult beetle flight + egg-laying peak in October–November with pre-monsoon showers. Heavy organic-residue plots amplify populations.

description.en: Soil-dwelling scarabaeid grubs that feed on roots of groundnut, sugarcane, coconut, and upland vegetables. Reported in Sri Lanka's groundnut and upland cropping systems. **Sri Lanka IPM recommendations**: (a) flood fields pre-sowing to kill grubs and deter egg-laying (viable where irrigation allows); (b) repeated ploughing in May–June to expose grubs to sun and bird predation; (c) avoid leaving organic matter/plant debris in the field between crops; (d) light traps during peak flight (October–November) to collect adult beetles; (e) hoeing/harrowing/trap cropping with sorghum, maize, or onion as border crops; (f) crop rotation with resistant crops (sunflower).

description.quote: White grub on ground nut-Sri Lanka.: Holotrichia sp. Flood the fields before sowing wherever possible to reduce the grub population. This avoid adult egg laying and kills the grubs in the soil. Plough the field repeatedly mainly in May-June to expose grubs to scorching sun radiation and for predation by natural enemies such as birds. Avoid keeping organic matter/ plant debris in field. Collect and destroy adult beetles by using light traps in peak periods (October-November).

favored_conditions.quote: Holotrichia serrata, commonly known as the sugarcane white grub, or cockchafer grub, is a species of dung beetle found in India, Bangladesh, and Sri Lanka. Apart from sugarcane, it is also a major pest on many vegetables, groundnut and coconut.

notes.en: Warm, dry conditions; sheltered plant canopies. Populations explode in the dry season and in greenhouse / polytunnel cultivation. Indiscriminate broad-spectrum insecticide use promotes resistance.

description.en: Tiny (1 mm) sap-sucking insects on the undersides of leaves that release a white waxy dust on disturbance. Direct feeding causes leaf yellowing, honeydew deposits that support sooty mould, and growth reduction. **More critically, Bemisia tabaci is the vector of several begomoviruses** — tomato yellow leaf curl virus (TYLCV), Sri Lankan cassava mosaic virus (SLCMV), leaf curl viruses of chilli and okra, and cucurbit yellows complexes — making whitefly management the primary lever for controlling these diseases. Management: reflective mulches, yellow sticky traps (4–5 per 100 m² of nursery), insect-proof nursery covers, biological control (*Encarsia formosa*, *Eretmocerus* parasitoids), selective insecticides (rotate MoA groups to delay resistance), and quick removal of alternate-host weeds.

description.quote: the silverleaf whitefly, Bemisia tabaci biotype B. Whiteflies can inoculate a tomato plant with TYLCV after 15 min of feeding

favored_conditions.quote: Only Asia II 1 whiteflies were able to transmit this virus efficiently among three widely distributed whitefly species in Asia

notes.en: Serious outbreaks are rare in Sri Lanka. Caterpillars bore into rice stems and hollow them out completely.

description.en: Caterpillars bore into rice stems. Young plants show 'dead hearts'; older plants show 'white heads' where panicles emerge empty. Affected stems are hollow and plants lodge easily. No resistant varieties available in Sri Lanka; cultural control via land preparation and weed management.

description.quote: Young plants display dead hearts; Older plants show white heads; Stem hollowing and lodging. The caterpillars bore into the rice stem and hollow out the stem completely.

favored_conditions.quote: Serious out breaks of YSB are very rare; resistant varieties are currently unavailable
